docs.title: dXon
docs.text:
dXon is a lightweight development container manager. instead of installing language toolchains and project dependencies directly on your host system, you create isolated containers for them. keeps things clean and reproducible.

it wraps system tools like systemd-nspawn, pacstrap, debootstrap, and Alpine's rootfs setup — no reinventing the wheel here, just making containers easier to use for everyday dev workflows.

docs.title: quick example
docs.text:
create a Node.js container from a template:

dxon create node-dev --template nodejs

enter it:

dxon enter node-dev

list all containers:

dxon list

delete one when you're done:

dxon delete node-dev

that's the core workflow. the Usage page covers all commands in detail.

docs.title: how it works
docs.text:
when you create a container, dXon:

  1. bootstraps a base Linux environment (Arch, Debian, or Alpine)
  2. installs packages defined by the template
  3. runs setup steps inside the container
  4. stores everything under ~/.dxon/containers/

from there you can enter the container any time with dxon enter.

docs.title: next steps
docs.text:
• Installation — get dXon on your system
  • Usage — full CLI reference
  • Templates — understand and write templates
  • Registry — community template registry
  • Configuration — configure dXon to your liking
  • Security — how template trust works

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
dxon stores its configuration file at:

$HOME/.config/dxon/config.toml

The file is created with defaults the first time dxon runs. View current settings with:

dxon config show

Change a value directly from the CLI:

dxon config set containers_dir /data/dxon/containers

Or edit the file directly with any text editor.

docs.title: containers_dir
docs.text:
Path where container filesystems are stored.

containers_dir = "/home/user/.dxon/containers"

Default: ~/.dxon/containers

docs.title: registry_url
docs.text:
URL of the template registry index. Change this to use a custom or self-hosted registry.

registry_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/P8labs/dxon-registry/main/registry.json"

Default: the official dxon registry index.

docs.title: default_distro
docs.text:
Pre-selects a base distribution in the interactive dxon create prompt.

default_distro = "arch"

Valid values: arch, debian, alpine.

docs.title: default_shell
docs.text:
Pre-selects a shell in the interactive dxon create prompt.

default_shell = "zsh"

Valid values: bash, zsh, fish.

docs.title: copy_shell_config
docs.text:
Pre-selects the shell config sharing mode in the interactive dxon create prompt.

copy_shell_config = "copy"

Valid values: copy, bind. See Shell config sharing for details on what each mode does.

docs.title: default_template
docs.text:
Pre-selects a template in the interactive dxon create prompt.

default_template = "rust"

docs.title: Full default configuration
docs.text:
containers_dir = "~/.dxon/containers"
registry_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/P8labs/dxon-registry/main/registry.json"

docs.title: Example: move container storage
docs.text:
If you want to store containers on a different drive or partition:

containers_dir = "/data/dxon/containers"

Make sure the directory exists and is writable before creating any containers.

docs.title: Example: use a custom registry
docs.text:
To use a private or self-hosted template registry:

registry_url = "https://registy.example.com/dxon/registry.json"

The registry endpoint must return a JSON index in the same format as the official registry. See Registry for details on the registry format.

docs.title: Prebuilt binaries
docs.text:
Prebuilt binaries for Linux (x86_64) are available on the GitHub releases page. Download the binary for your architecture, make it executable, and move it somewhere on your PATH:

chmod +x dxon
sudo mv dxon /usr/local/bin/

Verify the installation:

dxon --version

docs.title: Install from source
docs.text:
dXon is written in Rust. You'll need Cargo installed.

git clone https://github.com/P8labs/dxon.git
cd dxon
cargo build --release
sudo cp target/release/dxon /usr/local/bin/

docs.title: Install script
docs.text:
An install script is in the works. Once available it will let you install dXon with a single command:

curl -sSfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/P8labs/dxon/master/install.sh | sh

This script is not yet published.

docs.title: Package managers
docs.text:
Distribution packages are planned but not yet available. The following may be supported in the future:

  • AUR (Arch Linux) — dxon or dxon-bin
  • Other distro packages as interest grows

docs.title: Updating
docs.text:
To update dXon installed from source, pull the latest changes and rebuild:

cd dxon
git pull
cargo build --release
sudo cp target/release/dxon /usr/local/bin/

For prebuilt binaries, download the latest release and replace the existing binary.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
dXon relies on system tools to do the actual container work. Depending on which base distributions you want to use, you may need:

Distribution Required tool Arch Linux containers pacstrap (from arch-install-scripts) Debian/Ubuntu containers debootstrap Alpine containers wget or curl to fetch the Alpine rootfs

dXon will print a clear error if a required tool is missing when you try to create a container.

docs.title: Registry
docs.text:
The dXon template registry is a public collection of community-maintained environment templates. It lives at:

https://github.com/P8labs/dxon-registry

docs.title: Available templates
docs.text:
The registry currently includes:

Name Description nodejs Node.js with choice of npm, pnpm, yarn, or bun python Python 3 with pip rust Rust via rustup with clippy and rustfmt go Go development environment cpp C/C++ with build tools, optional cmake/ninja

List available templates directly from the CLI:

dxon template list

Search for a specific template:

dxon template search rust

docs.title: How registry resolution works
docs.text:
The registry uses an index file (registry.json) that maps template names to their locations. When you run:

dxon create myenv --template rust

dXon:

  1. Fetches the registry index from the configured registry URL
  2. Looks up the rust entry in the index
  3. Downloads the template YAML file
  4. Validates the template against the dxon/v1 schema
  5. Proceeds with container creation

Templates from the official registry are considered trusted and do not produce a security warning.

docs.title: Custom registry URL
docs.text: You can point dXon at a different registry by changing the registry URL in your configuration. The registry must expose a registry.json index with the same structure as the official one.

docs.title: Loading templates from a URL
docs.text:
You can load any template directly from a URL without publishing it to the registry:

dxon create myenv --template https://example.com/templates/myenv.yaml

Templates loaded this way are not considered trusted and will trigger a trust warning before the container is created.

docs.title: Loading templates from a local file
docs.text:
dxon create myenv --template ./myenv.yaml

Local file templates also trigger the trust warning. Use --trust (or -y) to skip it:

dxon create myenv --template ./myenv.yaml --trust

docs.title: Contributing templates
docs.text:
To add a template to the official registry, open a pull request on P8labs/dxon-registry. Templates should:

  • Follow the dxon/v1 schema
  • Work on at least one supported distribution
  • Include a brief description field

docs.title: Trusted templates
docs.text:
dXon distinguishes between trusted and untrusted templates based on where they come from.

Trusted sources:

  • Templates fetched from the official dXon registry (https://github.com/P8labs/dxon-registry)

Untrusted sources:

  • Templates loaded from a URL other than the official registry
  • Templates loaded from a local file path

docs.title: Trust warning
docs.text:
When you use a template from an untrusted source, dXon displays a warning before doing anything:

warning: this template is from an untrusted source
  source: https://example.com/templates/myenv.yaml

templates run commands inside your container during setup.
review the template before continuing.

do you want to proceed? [y/N]

You must confirm before dXon creates the container.

docs.title: Bypassing the prompt
docs.text:
If you have already reviewed the template and want to skip the confirmation, pass --trust (or -y):

dxon create myenv --template ./myenv.yaml --trust

This suppresses the prompt and proceeds directly. Use it in scripts or automation where interactive confirmation is not practical.

docs.title: Why this exists
docs.text:
Templates run arbitrary shell commands inside containers during setup. A malicious template could:

  • Install unwanted software
  • Exfiltrate files or credentials
  • Modify your container in unexpected ways

The trust system is a reminder to review templates from unknown sources before running them. Official registry templates are reviewed before publication, so they skip the prompt.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
• Prefer official registry templates when possible
  • Review template YAML before using --trust on an untrusted source
  • If you write templates for others to use, consider publishing them to the registry so they are reviewed and trusted by default

docs.title: Template Specification
docs.text: This page documents the complete dxon/v1 template format. Every field a template can contain is described here.

docs.title: File format
docs.text:
Templates are YAML files (.yaml or .yml) and must begin with the schema identifier:

schema: dxon/v1
name: myenv

Every field is optional except schema and name.

docs.title: Top-level fields
docs.text: Field Type Required Description schema string yes Must be dxon/v1 name string yes Short identifier shown in the registry, no spaces description string no One-line description of the environment base string no Suggested base distribution: arch, debian, or alpine packages map<distro, list<string>> no Per-distro package lists installed before steps run env map<string, string> no Environment variables set inside the container run list<string> no Commands run after all steps complete options list<Option> no Interactive prompts shown before container creation steps list<Step> no Ordered setup steps executed inside the container

docs.title: packages — per-distro package lists
docs.text:
Maps distribution names to raw package lists. Lets a single template work across Arch, Debian, and Alpine without relying on logical name translation.

packages:
  arch:   [curl, git, nodejs, npm]
  debian: [curl, git, ca-certificates, nodejs, npm]
  alpine: [curl, git, ca-certificates, nodejs, npm]

dXon selects the list matching the chosen distribution and installs those packages before running any steps. If no entry exists for the chosen distribution, the field is silently skipped.

Supported keys: arch, debian, alpine.

docs.title: env — environment variables
docs.text:
Variables set here apply during step execution and are preserved in the container metadata. They are applied every time you run dxon enter.

env:
  NODE_ENV: development
  GOPATH: /root/go
  CARGO_HOME: /root/.cargo

docs.title: run — post-step commands
docs.text:
Commands in run execute inside the container after all steps have completed. Useful for final configuration that depends on everything installed by steps.

run:
  - . /root/.cargo/env && rustup component add clippy rustfmt

docs.title: options — interactive prompts
docs.text: Options present the user with a choice before the container is created. The selected values can then gate individual steps via step when conditions.

docs.title: Option fields
docs.text:
Field Type Required Description id string yes Identifier referenced by step when conditions prompt string yes Question displayed to the user choices list<string> yes Valid answers; must be non-empty default string no Pre-selected answer; must appear in choices 
options:
  - id: pkg_manager
    prompt: "Which package manager would you like to use?"
    choices: [npm, pnpm, yarn, bun]
    default: npm

  - id: docker
    prompt: "Install Docker CLI tooling inside the container?"
    choices: ["no", "yes"]
    default: "no"

docs.title: steps — setup sequence
docs.text: Steps execute inside the container in order. Each step can install packages using logical tool names, run shell commands, or both.

docs.title: Step fields
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name string yes Display label shown in CLI output distro string no Restrict step to a single distro: arch, debian, or alpine tools list<string> no Logical tool names resolved to distro-specific packages run string or list<string> no Shell commands executed inside the container when map<option_id, value> no Run only when all listed option conditions match

docs.title: tools — logical tool names
docs.text:
Logical names are translated to the correct distro package automatically. Using them means your template works across all supported distributions without per-distro overrides.

Logical name arch debian alpine build-tools base-devel build-essential build-base cpp-compiler gcc g++ g++ c-compiler gcc gcc gcc ninja ninja ninja-build ninja go go golang-go go python3 python python3 python3 pip python-pip python3-pip py3-pip docker docker docker.io docker

docs.title: run — inline commands
docs.text:
run accepts a single string or a list of strings:

steps:
  - name: Install pnpm
    run: npm install -g pnpm

  - name: Configure Rust
    run:
      - . /root/.cargo/env && rustup default stable
      - rustup component add clippy rustfmt

docs.title: when — conditional execution
docs.text:
A step runs only when every entry in when matches the user's answer for that option. Steps without a when map always run.

steps:
  - name: Install pnpm
    run: npm install -g pnpm
    when:
      pkg_manager: pnpm

Multiple conditions must all match:

steps:
  - name: Setup with Docker
    run: echo "both matched"
    when:
      pkg_manager: pnpm
      docker: "yes"

docs.title: distro guard
docs.text:
Restricts a step to a single distribution. Useful when the setup procedure differs between distros:

steps:
  - name: Install Rust toolchain
    distro: arch
    run:
      - pacman -Sy --noconfirm rustup
      - rustup default stable

  - name: Install Rust toolchain
    distro: debian
    tools: [build-tools]
    run:
      - curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y --no-modify-path
      - . /root/.cargo/env && rustup default stable

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
dXon validates every template before container creation begins. Errors print clearly:

error: invalid template: options[0] 'pkg_manager': default 'bun' is not in choices ["npm", "pnpm", "yarn"]

Validation rules:

  • schema must equal dxon/v1
  • name must be non-empty
  • Each option must have a non-empty id and at least one choice
  • If default is set, it must appear in choices
  • Each step must have a non-empty name

docs.title: Complete example
docs.text:
schema: dxon/v1
name: nodejs
description: Node.js development environment

packages:
  arch:   [curl, git, nodejs, npm]
  debian: [curl, git, ca-certificates, nodejs, npm]
  alpine: [curl, git, ca-certificates, nodejs, npm]

env:
  NODE_ENV: development

options:
  - id: pkg_manager
    prompt: "Which package manager would you like to use?"
    choices: [npm, pnpm, yarn, bun]
    default: npm

  - id: docker
    prompt: "Install Docker CLI tooling inside the container?"
    choices: ["no", "yes"]
    default: "no"

steps:
  - name: Install pnpm
    run: npm install -g pnpm
    when:
      pkg_manager: pnpm

  - name: Install yarn
    run: npm install -g yarn
    when:
      pkg_manager: yarn

  - name: Install bun
    run: npm install -g bun
    when:
      pkg_manager: bun

  - name: Install Docker
    tools: [docker]
    when:
      docker: "yes"

docs.title: Templates
docs.text: Templates are YAML files that describe a complete development environment. They tell dXon which packages to install, which setup commands to run, and optionally which questions to ask you before creating the container.

docs.title: Template format
docs.text:
Templates use the dxon/v1 schema and are standard YAML files with a .yaml or .yml extension.

A minimal template looks like this:

schema: dxon/v1
name: myenv
description: My development environment

base: arch

packages:
  arch:
    - git
    - curl
    - nodejs
    - npm

steps:
  - name: Enable corepack
    run: corepack enable

docs.title: What a template contains
docs.text:
• base — the suggested base distro for the container (arch, debian, alpine)
  • packages — distribution-specific packages installed before any steps run
  • options — interactive prompts shown to the user before container creation
  • steps — ordered setup commands run inside the container after packages are installed
  • env — environment variables set inside the container
  • run — commands run after all steps complete

docs.title: From the registry
docs.text:
Reference a template by name and dXon fetches it from the official registry:

dxon create mynode --template nodejs
dxon create myrust --template rust --distro arch

docs.title: From a local file
docs.text: dxon create myenv --template ./myenv.yaml

docs.title: From a URL
docs.text:
dxon create myenv --template https://example.com/templates/myenv.yaml

Templates from sources other than the official registry will show a trust warning before proceeding.

docs.title: Official templates
docs.text:
The official registry includes templates for common environments:

Name Description nodejs Node.js with optional package manager (npm, pnpm, yarn, bun) python Python 3 with pip rust Rust via rustup with clippy and rustfmt go Go development environment cpp C/C++ with build tools and optional cmake/ninja

Pull the full list with:

dxon template list

docs.title: Writing your own template
docs.text:
1. Create a file with a .yaml extension
  2. Set schema: dxon/v1 and give it a name
  3. Define packages, options, and steps as needed
  4. Test locally: dxon create test --template ./mytemplate.yaml

The Template Specification documents every field in detail.

docs.title: Creating a container
docs.text:
The main command is dxon create. At minimum you provide a name for the container:

dxon create myenv

This starts an interactive prompt where you choose the base distro, shell, and optionally a template.

docs.title: Non-interactive creation
docs.text:
Use flags to skip the prompts:

dxon create myenv --distro arch
dxon create myenv --distro debian --template python
dxon create myenv --distro alpine --template nodejs

Flags:

Flag Description --distro <name> Base distribution: arch, debian, or alpine --template <name> Template name from the registry, a local file path, or a URL --repo <url> Clone a git repository into the container after setup --shell <name> Shell to install: bash, zsh, or fish --shell-config <mode> Share host shell config: copy or bind --trust, -y Skip confirmation prompts (including trust warnings for untrusted templates)

docs.title: Example: clone a repo into a container
docs.text:
dxon create myproject --distro arch --template rust --repo https://github.com/you/myproject

The repository is cloned into /workspace inside the container after setup completes.

docs.title: Shell selection
docs.text:
During dxon create you can choose which shell to install in the container: bash, zsh, or fish. The chosen shell becomes the default for dxon enter.

dxon create myenv --distro arch --shell zsh

docs.title: Sharing host shell config
docs.text:
You can bring your host shell configuration into the container with --shell-config:

Mode Behaviour copy Shell config files are copied into the container once at creation time. Paths referencing your home directory are rewritten to /root. bind Shell config files are bind-mounted from the host every time you enter. Changes on the host are immediately reflected inside the container. 
dxon create myenv --distro arch --shell zsh --shell-config copy
dxon create myenv --distro arch --shell zsh --shell-config bind

Files copied/bound per shell:

  • bash — .profile, .bash_profile, .bash_login, .bashrc, .bash_aliases, .bash_logout, .inputrc
  • zsh — .zshenv, .zshrc, .zprofile, .zlogin, .zlogout, .inputrc (respects $ZDOTDIR)
  • fish — ~/.config/fish/ directory (respects $XDG_CONFIG_HOME)

docs.title: Entering a container
docs.text:
dxon enter myenv

This drops you into an interactive shell inside the container using the shell that was chosen at creation time. If no shell was configured, bash is used.

docs.title: Opening a container in an editor
docs.text:
dxon open myenv

Opens the container's /workspace directory (or root filesystem if no workspace exists) in a supported code editor. Editors are detected automatically in this order: VS Code (code), Cursor (cursor), Zed (zed).

To use a specific editor:

dxon open myenv --editor cursor
dxon open myenv --editor zed

docs.title: VS Code / Cursor terminal integration
docs.text:
When opening with VS Code or Cursor, dXon automatically writes a .vscode/settings.json inside the workspace that configures a dXon terminal profile. This profile runs dxon enter <name> so that every integrated terminal you open is already inside the container.

The profile is set as the default terminal, so pressing Ctrl+` drops you directly into the container shell.

docs.title: Zed
docs.text: Zed does not yet support automatic terminal profile injection. Use dxon enter <name> from a host terminal alongside Zed.

docs.title: Listing containers
docs.text:
dxon list

Prints all containers with their name, base distro, and creation date.

docs.title: Inspecting a container
docs.text:
dxon info myenv

Shows details about a container: base distro, template used, creation time, any environment variables, and the storage path.

docs.title: Deleting a container
docs.text:
dxon delete myenv

Permanently removes the container and all its files. This cannot be undone.

docs.title: Running commands
docs.text:
To run a single command inside a container without entering an interactive shell:

dxon enter myenv -- cargo build --release

docs.title: Template commands
docs.text:
Inspect the template registry:

dxon template list
dxon template search nodejs
dxon template refresh

See Registry for more.

docs.title: Configuration commands
docs.text:
dxon config show
dxon config set containers_dir /data/dxon/containers

docs.title: Global flags
docs.text: Flag Description --help Show help for any command --version Print the dXon version
